prompt_md: You open a file with `std::ifstream f("data.txt")` but the file does not exist. What happens to subsequent reads from `f`?

options.b: `failbit` is set and all subsequent reads silently do nothing; variables keep their prior values.
options.c: The program creates an empty `data.txt` and reads from it successfully.
options.d: `badbit` is set and the program terminates.

explanation_md: Opening a non-existent file with `std::ifstream` sets `failbit`. No exception is thrown by default, and no file is created. Subsequent reads check the flag and silently skip, leaving variables at whatever value they held before. Always check `if (!f)` right after opening.

prompt_md: Which open mode should you use to add new lines to an existing file without deleting its current content?

options.b: `std::ios::in | std::ios::out` â€” opens for both reading and writing.
options.c: `std::ios::app` â€” all writes go to the end of the file.
options.d: `std::ios::trunc` â€” truncate then write from the beginning.

explanation_md: `std::ios::app` (append mode) positions the write pointer at the end of the file before every write, so existing content is preserved. `std::ios::out` without `app` truncates the file to zero bytes on open. `std::ios::trunc` explicitly truncates.

prompt_md: Why can a function with signature `int countLines(std::istream& in)` be called with both `std::cin` and an `std::ifstream` object?

options.a: Because `std::cin` and `std::ifstream` are both global objects.
options.b: Because `std::ifstream` inherits from `std::istream`, so an `std::ifstream` IS an `std::istream`.
options.c: Because `std::istream&` is a template that deduces the argument type.
options.d: Because C++ implicitly converts any stream to `std::istream` via a cast operator.

explanation_md: `std::ifstream` inherits from `std::istream`. A reference to a base class can bind to a derived-class object, so passing an `std::ifstream` where `std::istream&` is expected is valid. This is the same polymorphism that lets `std::ostringstream` accept all `<iomanip>` manipulators.
`std::getline` reads `"hello"` (count becomes 1), then `"world"` (count becomes 2), then hits EOF and the loop exits. The program prints `2`.
